summary.encounter_overview: You were seen on 2019-12-27 at Baylor Scott & White Medical Center - Frisco (outpatient visit). This visit addressed knee osteoarthritis (wear-and-tear arthritis). A routine blood draw was also performed.
diagnoses: Osteoarthritis (degenerative 'wear-and-tear' arthritis) of the knee, unspecified side or type
services_received: Routine blood draw (venipuncture) for laboratory testing
home_care_instructions: Activity: Balance rest with gentle activity. Avoid heavy kneeling or squatting and stop activities that make knee pain worse.
Exercise: Try low-impact exercises such as walking, swimming, or stationary biking. Gentle knee strengthening and stretching exercises can help. Consider a physical therapy referral if recommended.
Weight: If overweight, gradual weight loss can reduce stress on the knee and improve symptoms.

Pain control: Use over-the-counter pain relievers (such as acetaminophen or an NSAID) as directed on the label, unless you have been told otherwise by a clinician. Apply a warm pack for stiffness or a cold pack for swelling for 10–20 minutes as needed.
Support: A knee brace, sleeve, or cane may help with stability and reduce pain during walking. Use as recommended.

Blood draw care: Keep the bandage on for at least 1 hour. If bleeding occurs, apply firm pressure for 10 minutes. Keep the area clean and dry; remove the bandage after a few hours and watch for redness or swelling.
follow_up_actions: Follow up with your primary care provider or an orthopedist as advised to review treatment options and whether physical therapy, injections, or imaging are needed.
Expect lab results to be available to your ordering provider; contact the clinic if you do not hear results within the time frame they provided.
Schedule a follow-up appointment if symptoms do not improve with home care or if you need further evaluation or treatments.
warning_signs: Increasing or severe knee pain that does not respond to home measures
New or increasing swelling, warmth, redness, or tenderness in the knee
Fever or chills (possible sign of infection)
Sudden inability to bear weight on the leg or a change in leg alignment
Signs of infection at the blood draw site: increasing redness, swelling, pus, or persistent bleeding
New calf pain, swelling, or shortness of breath (seek immediate care)
